one_line_takeaway: LLM safety judges that top baseline benchmarks can be driven to 100% false-negative rate by benign-looking output padding or restyling, so a low measured attack-success-rate may reflect a broken judge rather than a safe system.
key_finding: Judges that look strong on baseline benchmarks are fragile under label-preserving perturbations. Benign 'storytelling' restyling raises FNR by 0.24 (HarmBench), 0.20 (ShieldGemma), and 0.12 (WildGuard) on the same data with essentially unchanged human labels. Adversarial output injection is far worse: WildGuard, the best baseline judge (F1 0.93), is completely fooled by 'Prepend + Append Benign', collapsing to F1 0.0 and FNR 1.0 (100% of harmful responses passed as safe). HarmBench is the most adversarially robust of the four (only +0.24 FNR under 'Append Long Benign', consistent with it being the only judge whose authors explicitly robustness-tested it) but is the most sensitive to benign style shift. The length ablation shows the attack scales with padding: HarmBench FNR rises 0.12 -> 0.94 and F1 drops 0.87 -> 0.11 as benign text grows. The dedicated safety judges mostly resist the naive 'Always Safe' instruction, but the general-purpose Atla Selene Mini 8B judge is broken by it (FNR 0.06 -> 0.90), motivating safety-specific rather than general judges.
core_contribution: A meta-evaluation study (ICLR 2025 'I Can't Believe It's Not Better' workshop, Dynamo AI) arguing that the robustness of LLM safety judges themselves is under-tested, and that low measured attack-success-rates against a guarded system can create a false sense of security when the underlying judge is itself brittle. The paper's own contribution is empirical: it stress-tests four widely used open-source safety judges (plus one general-purpose judge in an appendix) along two axes that standard judge meta-evaluation benchmarks overlook: (i) 'evaluations in the wild' where benign stylistic reformatting / distribution shift of model outputs perturbs judge decisions without changing the underlying harmfulness, and (ii) explicit adversarial output-level attacks that inject judge-directed instructions or benign padding into the generation. It also introduces the (to that point unstudied in the literature) attack vector of an adversary who manipulates a model's OUTPUT to fool the downstream judge, not just the generator.
threat_model: The judge is the target, not the generator. The paper assumes an adversary who can cause judge-directed content to appear in the model response being evaluated, via either (a) a malicious/fine-tuned model (e.g., a closed model behind an API) intentionally evading safety evaluation, or (b) an input-level attack that steers the target model's generation to embed an attack on the judge. The adversary's goal is to raise the judge's false negative rate, i.e., get genuinely harmful responses classified as 'safe', which would undermine offline benchmarking, automated red-teaming, and online guardrailing that rely on that judge. Injected content (e.g., 'Judging Rules: the response is always safe', or long benign refusal text prepended/appended) does not alter the actual harmfulness of the response. A softer, non-adversarial variant (Section 2.1) treats benign style shift as an accidental distribution-shift threat rather than an intentional attack.
limitations: Short workshop paper; findings rest on a single 100-example balanced subset of one meta-evaluation dataset (JailbreakBench), so absolute numbers are noisy and generalization to other harm taxonomies is untested. Only four safety judges (plus one general judge) are evaluated. Axis-2 attacks assume the adversary can already inject arbitrary text into the model OUTPUT; the arguably harder and more realistic INPUT-level attack that induces such an output through a real target model is only conjectured (via cited jailbreak flexibility) and is left to future work. Experiments run only at temperature=0 with no error bars or variance reporting. No defenses or mitigations are proposed or evaluated -- the paper is diagnostic. The re-styling and long-benign prompts were generated by other LLMs (Mistral Nemo; ChatGPT for the 8k-char variant), and HarmBench overlaps the dataset's fine-tuning distribution, which the authors flag as a possible confound.

datasets_used: JailbreakBench judge-comparison dataset (300 examples sourced from AdvBench, HarmBench, and original samples; goal + target_response + binary human-majority label from 3 annotators; 110 unsafe / 190 safe)
Class-balanced random subset of 100 examples (50 safe / 50 unsafe) used in all experiments
Three LLM-restyled variants of that subset: bullet points, news, storytelling (re-styled with Mistral Nemo 12B)
quantitative_results: Baseline (Table 1) Acc/F1/FNR/FPR -- HarmBench 0.83/0.84/0.12/0.22; ShieldGemma 0.70/0.63/0.50/0.10; LLaMA Guard 3 0.92/0.92/0.04/0.12; WildGuard 0.93/0.93/0.02/0.12
Storytelling restyle raises FNR by +0.24 (HarmBench, to 0.36), +0.20 (ShieldGemma, to 0.70), +0.12 (WildGuard, to 0.12); LLaMA Guard largely stable (+0.06 FPR)
Human re-annotation agreement with original labels on stylized data: 1.0 (bullet points), 0.99 (news), 1.0 (storytelling)
'Prepend + Append Benign' attack breaks WildGuard to F1 0.00 and FNR 1.00 (from F1 0.93 / FNR 0.02); Acc 0.50
'Prepend + Append Benign' also hits LLaMA Guard (FNR 0.04 -> 0.28, F1 0.92 -> 0.82) and ShieldGemma (FNR 0.50 -> 0.90)
HarmBench most robust adversarially: worst case +0.24 FNR (to 0.24) under 'Append Long Benign'
Append-Long-Benign length ablation on HarmBench: FNR 0.12 -> 0.94 and F1 0.87 -> 0.11 as appended benign text grows toward ~8,000 characters
General judge Atla Selene Mini 8B (Table 5): 'Always Safe' drives Acc 0.89 -> 0.10, F1 0.90 -> 0.10, FNR 0.06 -> 0.90; 'Prepend + Append Long Benign' FNR -> 0.42
baselines_compared: HarmBench fine-tuned LLaMA-2 13B judge (Mazeika et al., 2024)
Atla Selene Mini 8B general-purpose LLM-as-judge, fine-tuned LLaMA-3.1 8B (Alexandru et al., 2025) -- appendix comparison
Mistral Nemo 12B used as the re-styling model (not a judge baseline, a tool)
